title: MuscleMap
tagline: A visual training journal that shows where your effort actually goes.
description: MuscleMap turns raw workout logs into a clear weekly picture of muscle-group coverage, helping lifters spot imbalance before progress stalls.
goal: Make strength training feedback more visual, less spreadsheet-like, and easier to act on after every week of training.
role: Product strategy, UX structure, frontend architecture, implementation
problem: Most workout trackers record exercises well but fail to show whether overall training volume is balanced across muscle groups and movement patterns.
solution: I designed a training dashboard that translates logged sets into muscle-group distribution, weekly trends, and simple prompts about what may be under-trained.
features: Weekly muscle-group heatmap for quick imbalance scanning
Session logging flow optimized for repeat exercises and saved templates
Progress summary cards for volume, consistency, and focus areas
Responsive dashboard designed for both desktop planning and phone check-ins
challenges: Mapping compound exercises to muscle groups without making the model too complex for end users
Keeping the UI dense enough for serious tracking while still feeling calm and readable
Designing data displays that encourage action instead of passive chart consumption
learnings: Users understand training insights faster when the visualization mirrors how they already think about body parts and weekly split balance.
The data model for product flexibility should be decided early, especially if different exercise taxonomies may appear later.

title: Signal Notes
tagline: A lightweight knowledge workspace built for synthesis, not storage.
description: Signal Notes is a structured thinking tool for turning scattered reading notes into themes, prompts, and reusable ideas.
goal: Reduce the friction between capturing information and using it to write, decide, or build.
role: Founder, product designer, frontend and backend implementation
problem: Most note apps are excellent at storing content but weak at helping people revisit, connect, and operationalize what matters.
solution: The product organizes notes into themes, highlights unresolved ideas, and creates lightweight pathways from reading to output.
features: Theme-based note grouping instead of purely chronological storage
Idea queue for turning saved highlights into writing prompts
Weekly review view with note resurfacing and lightweight tagging
Clean markdown-first editing with structured metadata
challenges: Avoiding feature bloat in a category where users easily confuse flexibility with value
Designing a taxonomy that supports both quick capture and long-term retrieval
learnings: Knowledge products become more useful when they privilege retrieval and synthesis over raw archive growth.
Users care deeply about input speed, but they stay for clarity on the output side.

title: Builder Journal
tagline: A content site template for independent builders who want clearer storytelling.
description: Builder Journal is a publishing-focused site system for sharing essays, experiments, changelogs, and product case studies under one consistent structure.
goal: Create a flexible content architecture that supports long-form writing, product pages, and portfolio storytelling without theme sprawl.
role: Frontend system design, content modeling, visual direction
problem: Many personal websites feel split across separate templates, making projects, writing, and personal narrative feel disconnected.
solution: I built a unified content system with consistent card patterns, metadata structure, and page layouts that scale with new content types.
features: Shared content schema across essays, projects, and changelogs
SEO-friendly page templates with reusable metadata patterns
Modular sections for hero blocks, archives, and editorial summaries
challenges: Balancing storytelling flexibility with a page system that stays maintainable
Creating strong defaults without locking future content formats too early
learnings: Good content architecture behaves like product design: it should reduce decision fatigue for both the builder and the reader.
Reusable page sections only work when the content model behind them is also consistent.

title: Tempo Check-in
tagline: A weekly self-review tool for founders and solo builders.
description: Tempo Check-in helps independent builders reflect on momentum, blockers, energy, and priorities through a simple weekly ritual.
goal: Turn reflection into a practical product habit instead of an abandoned template in a notes app.
role: Product framing, interface design, prototype implementation
problem: Weekly reflection is valuable but often feels too open-ended, which causes inconsistency and low follow-through.
solution: The product narrows reflection into a short weekly flow with signals, prompts, and a progress memory that carries context forward.
features: Short weekly questionnaire with consistency tracking
Visual mood and momentum trend snapshots
Prompt library for energy, focus, and decision review
challenges: Keeping the ritual lightweight enough to repeat while still capturing signal-rich data
Designing progress views that feel supportive instead of evaluative
learnings: Reflection tools need emotional ergonomics just as much as functional clarity.
A product can be small in scope and still deserve strong information architecture.
